statement: Database access is encapsulated behind repository (or DAO) functions, one per logical entity, exposing intention-revealing operations rather than raw queries.
structure: `UserRepository.findByEmail(email)`, `UserRepository.deactivate(userId)`. Repositories return domain objects, not raw rows. They do not contain business rules — those live in services. They are the only layer that knows the schema.
why: Schema changes touch one file instead of many. The query catalog is discoverable: `grep` the repository to find every way users are fetched. Mocking is trivial in service tests. Caching, instrumentation, and read/write splitting attach at the repository boundary instead of being scattered.
anti_pattern: Inline queries in handlers (see antipattern:direct-db-from-handler). Repository methods that return ORM query builders instead of finalized results — leaks the abstraction.
